title: Circling back…
excerpt: The things I turn to again and again.
content:
The things I turn to again and again.

“The Last Lecture” by Randy Pausch

I’m not a huge fan of the self-help genre and the plethora of “gurus” out there writing manifestos on how to lead your life. I am a big fan of thoughtful reflections on one’s life that can teach us a thing or two about how to live out our own. This is that. I love to rewatch this lecture every year or so. It reminds me to never forget your childhood dreams (12-year old you was way cooler than you give them credit for) and so much more.

“Little Miss Sunshine”

There’s this really cool video analysis here that explains why the creator returns to the movie when they are sad and that is precisely what keeps me coming back to it as well. Keep pushing that yellow Volkswagen :))

“Dancing Queen” by ABBA

This needs no explanation.

title: On indecision and “locking in”…
excerpt: Hunched over my EE140 Lab in a dimly-lit Cory lab, I was redoing my schematic for the 5th time when my friend sitting behind me (let’s call him A,) posed a q…
content:
Hunched over my EE140 Lab in a dimly-lit Cory lab, I was redoing my schematic for the 5th time when my friend sitting behind me (let’s call him A,) posed a question that made me stop my rapid typing and think long and hard for a moment:

“I don’t know,” he said. “I don’t want to lock myself into one thing, one area for the rest of my career, you know? That just sounds so awful. What do you think, Sanjana?”

I looked up from my hopelessly erroring testbench and paused. His question, or at least a variation of it, had been playing in the back of my head for a while now as I’ve progressed through my third year at Berkeley. In a way, I suppose that it had always been there–this nagging voice in my head that’s always reminded me of my fatal flaw, my achilles heel: Hopeless Indecision.

A had decided to accept a job offer, but he was worried that he would be boxing himself in. I had thought about his comment or a moment and replied.

“I don’t think you can ever really box yourself in. You’re just starting out, after all. If you decide something is not for you, I suppose you can always pivot.”

But even as I articulated those words out loud, I could already see three pitiful words flash before my eyes.

What. A. Hypocrite.

Who was I to reassure A like that? Ha–I wasn’t even following my own words of advice. It really is far easier to dish out advice than to receive it.

Since I’ve started college, my mantra has been to try everything (within reason) and to be open to new opportunities and fields. I figured that if I had a solid goal of finding what interested and excited me, I would eventually find the right subject that would compel me to dive deeper.. Sounds simple, right? While this mindset has certainly provided me with a breadth of interests and experiences, there’s also this double edge to this benefit: I feel like trying everything has also limited me. As the saying goes……

“A jack of all trades, is a master of none…”

But even that isn’t the complete truth. The complete saying goes:

“… but oftentimes better than a master of one.”

See? Indecision, indecision, indecision. It’s plagued me ever since I received my college acceptance and was faced with the reality that I now had to fully decide what I wanted to focus on. . The key word in the previous sentence? Focus.. Focus, focus, focus is my dad’s mantra and his main source of disapproval. He thought that I was spreading myself too thin..

And honestly, I can’t really disagree with him here. I’ve found myself pulled in so many directions that I’ve never been able to decide which one I wanted to do. I can’t even convince myself to call it “exploration”; After all, even old world explorers had sat down to draw some lines on their map, no matter how inaccurate or arbitrary their decisions were. I couldn’t say the same thing about myself..

Freshman year, there was a running joke that I was “pre-everything.” Pre-law, pre-med, pre-grad school, pre-engineering.. This manifested in a rather bizarre collection of classes I took and side-quest summers where I explored one new area after another. I just couldn’t help myself! I had stuck to a rather narrow definition of what “success” meant all throughout high school (grades, test scores, standard ECs), and for the first time, I had the freedom to define what success meant to me. Hardware, software, ML, biotech. Grad school, industry, startups, (cow herder?). Oh, the choices!

One summer, my Bay-Area-confined-ass fucked off to Singapore to model genetic circuits and program vertical famring robots while navigating South East Asia. The Summer after, I delved into the field of LLM research, scouring over differential privacy and the trustworthiness of AI. After work, I would take my company shuttle across the Bay to a “hacker house” with biotech enthusiasts who would explain to me their latest endeavors or biology-related rants (like what a plasmid is). This summer, I’ll be doing deep learning computer architecture for AI chips while simultaneously pursuing a particle physics research stint in Switzerland. I have enjoyed it all (a little too much I’ll admit), but at some point, I’m going to have to choose which path I go down. As my Dad likes to remind me, you need focus, Sanjana. Focus.

As I near senior year, I’ve found that my pursuits of “exploration” have brewed a sense of quiet anxiety, primarily stemming around the question of “What if I don’t truly know what I’m interested in,” “What if I’m just saying that I am something when I’m not,” and “Would I think differently if I wasn’t in X, at Y, or studying Z?”

More explicitly (especially as I map out my Summer plans and next year), I think to myself, am I making the right choice?

I’ll admit, this issue comes from a place of privilege during a time when many people don’t even have the luxury of choice. It’s a silly dilemma to have in the grand scheme of things. I know that. But as one of my favorite YouTubers, John Green, says,

“…we don’t live in the grand scheme of things”

With a general lack of fortune telling abilities, humans often wonder, Did I make the right decision, a lot. I certainly know I do.

I think I’ve come to realize that while this is a valid question to ask, it’s an invalid question to leave hanging. I’m ultimately the one who determines to perceive something as the right decision. And it’s truly up to me to make it the right one.

I don’t think I fully internalized this concept more than last Summer, when I had the opportunity to join Y Combinator in one of their batches in exchange for leaving my current job and “temporarily” leaving school to go “all in” on an idea.

A parent’s worst nightmare, but a reality not uncommon in the Silicon Valley start-up culture that permeated throughout my college campus, is dropping out of school and joining YC.

I considered my options, made my mental calculations, and ultimately determined that the best place for me to learn (and grow) was to complete my internship and stay in school. I declined the YC offer.

I think a past version of me would have replayed those simulations and entered “analysis paralysis” for a much longer period of time. I would say that I surprised myself a bit by not looking back since. Is this what they call the frontal lobe developing? I sure hope so.

In the end, maybe we are all just gaslighting ourselves. But then again, how much do we really know what we truly want? Is everything we tell ourselves a lie? And the truth is just what we choose to believe? Perhaps the key is understanding that there isn’t a clear cut boundary to who we are and the “lies” we tell ourselves. That with enough time, they form an amalgamation that we call “me.” So, it’s best to make a choice and trust in that choice, despite the underlying fear (of failure, of discomfort, of judgement). After all, I believe that that’s what forced me to be stuck in this state of indecision; it’s a limbo that removes risk; parroting as “potential” but quickly morphing into inaction.

Anyways… Those are my thoughts towards the end of the midterms. Now, I’m looking into the multiple hours of studying that awaits me for finals.

Damn. I should really lock in.

title: 2026 Reading
excerpt: Here’s a running list of books I’ve read this year and quotes I particulary enjoyed from them :)
content:
Here’s a running list of books I’ve read this year and quotes I particulary enjoyed from them :)

Everything is Tuberculosis — John Green:

  • “While I’m fascinated by how TB shaped culture and history, what’s most important to me is how culture has shaped TB. The infection has long exploited human biases and blind spots, wriggling its way through the paths injustice creates.”
  • “The way we symbolize disease ends up shaping the way we experience and respond to disease.”
  • “We can do and be so much for each other — but only when we see one another in our full humanity, not as statistics or problems, but as people who deserve to be alive in the world.”
  • “We are powerful enough to light the world at night, to artificially refrigerate food, to leave Earth’s atmosphere and orbit it from outer space. But we cannot save those we love from suffering. This is the story of human history as I understand it — the story of an organism that can do so much, but cannot do what it most wants.”
  • “But as a friend once told me, “Nothing is so privileged as thinking history belongs to the past.”

The God of Small Things — Arundhati Roy

  • “…the secret of the Great Stories is that they have no secrets. The Great Stories are the ones you have heard and want to hear again. The ones you can enter anywhere and inhabit comfortably. They don’t deceive you with thrills and trick endings. They don’t surprise you with the unforeseen. They are as familiar as the house you live in. Or the smell of your lover’s skin. You know how they end, yet you listen as though you don’t. In the way that although you know that one day you will die, you live as though you won’t. In the Great Stories you know who lives, who dies, who finds love, who doesn’t. And yet you want to know again. That is their mystery and their magic.”
  • “And the air was full of Thoughts and Things to Say. But at times like these, only the Small Things are ever said. Big Things lurk unsaid inside.”
  • “He folded his fear into a perfect rose. He held it out in the palm of his hand. She took it from him and put it in her hair.”
  • “There is a war that makes us adore our conquerors and despise ourselves.”
  • “There is a war that makes us adore our conquerors and despise ourselves.”
  • “Ammu said that human beings were creatures of habit, and it was amazing the kind of things one could get used to
  • “Things can change in a day.”
  • “Little events, ordinary things, smashed and reconstituted. Suddenly, they become the bleached bones of a story.”
  • “And there it was again. Another religion turned against itself. Another edifice constructed by the human mind, decimated by human nature.”
  • “There are things that you can’t do — like writing letters to a part of yourself. To your feet or hair. Or heart.”
  • “Stories reveal themselves to us. The public narrative, the private narrative — they colonize us. They commission us. They insist on being told. Fiction and nonfiction are only different techniques of story telling. For reasons that I don’t fully understand, fiction dances out of me, and nonfiction is wrenched out by the aching, broken world I wake up to every morning.”

Kara and The Sun — Kazuo Ishiguro

  • “Yes. Until recently, I didn’t think that humans could choose loneliness. That there were sometimes forces more powerful than the wish to avoid loneliness.”
  • “In the morning when the Sun returns. It’s possible for us to hope.”
  • “Our generation still carry the old feelings. A part of us refuses to let go. The part that wants to keep believing there’s something unreachable inside each of us. Something that’s unique and won’t transfer. But there’s nothing like that, we know that now. You know that. For people our age it’s a hard one to let go.”

The Kite Runner — Khaled Hosseini

  • “It may be unfair, but what happens in a few days, sometimes even a single day, can change the course of a whole lifetime…”
  • “And that’s the thing about people who mean everything they say. They think everyone else does too.”
  • “There is only one sin. and that is theft… when you tell a lie, you steal someones right to the truth.”
  • “People say that eyes are windows to the soul.”
  • “Time can be a greedy thing-sometimes it steals the details for itself.”
  • “A man who has no conscience, no goodness, does not suffer.”
  • “Better to get hurt by the truth than comforted with a lie.”
  • “Sad stories make good books.”
  • “For you a thousand times over.”

Demon Copperhead — Barbara Kingsolver

  • “Live long enough, and all things you ever loved can turn around to scorch you blind. The wonder is that you could start life with nothing, end with nothing, and lose so much in between.”
  • “a good story doesn’t just copy life, it pushes back on it.”
  • “I got up every day thinking the sun was out there shining, and it could just as well shine on me as any other human person.”
  • “Keeping secrets from young ears only plants seeds in between them,”

Orbital — Samatha Harvey

  • “The earth, from here, is like heaven. It flows with colour. A burst of hopeful colour. When we’re on that planet we look up and think heaven is elsewhere, but here is what the astronauts and cosmonauts sometimes think: maybe all of us born to it have already died and are in an afterlife. If we must go to an improbable, hard-to-believe-in place when we die, that glassy, distant orb with its beautiful lonely light shows could well be it.”
  • “Our lives here are inexpressibly trivial and momentous at once, it seems he’s about to wake up and say. Both repetitive and unprecedented. We matter greatly and not at all. To reach some pinnacle of human achievement only to discover that your achievements are next to nothing and that to understand this is the greatest achievement of any life, which itself is nothing, and also much more than everything. Some metal separates us from the void; death is so close. Life is everywhere, everywhere.”
  • “Because who can look at man’s neurotic assault on the planet and find it beautiful? Man’s hubris. A hubris so almighty it’s matched only by his stupidity.”
  • “And in time we come to see that not only are we on the sidelines of the universe but that it’s of a universe of sidelines, that there is no centre, just a giddy mass of waltzing things, and that perhaps the entirety of our understanding consists of an elaborate and ever-evolving knowledge of our own extraneousness, a bashing away of mankind’s ego by the instruments of scientific enquiry until it is, that ego, a shattered edifice that lets light through.”
  • “There are people like him (so he says) who complicate their inner lives by feeling too much all at once, by living in knots, and who therefore need outer things to be simple. A house, a field, some sheep for example. And there are those who manage somehow, by some miracle of being, to simplify their inner lives so that outer things can be ambitious and limitless. Those people can swap out a house for a spaceship, a field for a universe.”

Intermittently in Progress:

  • Fight Club — Chuck Palahniuk
  • Martyr! — Kaveh Akbar
  • Dune — Frank Herbert
  • The Empusium — Olga Tokarczuk

title: Chronic Drafting
excerpt: For the longest time it took forever for my ideas, plans, and half-baked emails to make it out of that confining folder titled “drafts”. Here’s what I did to…
content:
For the longest time it took forever for my ideas, plans, and half-baked emails to make it out of that confining folder titled “drafts”. Here’s what I did to fix it:

In the spirit of hypocrisy here are all the medium articles I started and did not (and will not) finish:

title: 2025 Reading
excerpt: An attempt to preserve my 12 year-old self’s reading habits—
content:
An attempt to preserve my 12 year-old self’s reading habits—

Katabasis — R.F. Kuang:

  • “And if falling in love was discovery, was letting yourself be discovered the equivalent to being loved?”
  • “Hell’s lonely,” said Peter. “You’ll want company.” “Hell is other people, I’ve heard.”
  • “You’ve got that look about you.” “What look?” “Well, not to be rude, but you’re all fucked up, aren’t you?”
  • “This was the key to flourishing in graduate school. You could do anything if you were delusional.”
  • “Sometimes I am very clever but most of the time I am not. I have been a good person sometimes, and a bad person at others. Sooner or later I will die. But before I do, I will try — I will try very hard — to make it count.”
  • “Everyone knew that the nicer a library was, the better the work you did within it.”
  • “The world is not a complete system; there is always an exception. No explanation for its existence; no reason why one might expect it to have existed before or to ever exist again. The world was simply unknowable; exceptions cropped up all the time, and all you had to do to beat the odds was just look.”
  • “And so perhaps it was entirely possible — common, even — for you to look into the eyes of someone you’d been falling in love with, someone you had spent every waking moment with, whose breathing sounded as familiar as your own — and fail to recognize them at all.”

Intermezzo — Sally Rooney:

  • How grief impacts relationships both familial and romantic and the dangers of holding onto something that has fundamentally changed
  • “Yes, the world makes room for goodness and decency, he thinks: and the task of life is to show goodness to others, not to complain about their failings.”
  • “what if life is just a collection of essentially unrelated experiences? Why does one thing have to follow meaningfully from another?”
  • “Sometimes you need people to be perfect and they can’t be and you hate them forever for not being even though it isn’t their fault and it’s not yours either. You just needed something they didn’t have in them to give you.”
  • “We’re being hard on ourselves in a way, he remarks, because both our lives involve some voluntary exposure to what other people might call defeat. Which I think requires a certain degree of courage.”
  • “There is more to life than great chess. Okay, great chess is still a part of life, and it can be a very big part, very intense, satisfying, and pleasant to dwell on in the mind’s eye: but nonetheless, life contains many things. Life itself, he thinks, every moment of life, is as precious and beautiful as any game of chess ever played, if only you know how to live.”

Lessons in Chemistry — Bonnie Garmus:

  • I admired the determination of the main character driving home the point that no one is going to change your life for you, except you
  • “Whenever you feel afraid, just remember. Courage is the root of change — and change is what we’re chemically designed to do. So when you wake up tomorrow, make this pledge. No more holding yourself back. No more subscribing to others’ opinions of what you can and cannot achieve. And no more allowing anyone to pigeonhole you into useless categories of sex, race, economic status, and religion. Do not allow your talents to lie dormant, ladies. Design your own future. When you go home today, ask yourself what YOU will change. And then get started.”
  • “(On religion) “I think it lets us off the hook. I think it teaches us that nothing is really our fault; that something or someone else is pulling the strings; the ultimately, we’re not to blame for the way things are; that to improve things, we should pray. But the truth is, we are very much responsible for the badness in the world. And we have the power to fix it.”
  • “some things needed to stay in the past because the past was the only place they made sense.”
  • “Chemistry is change and change is the core of your belief system. Which is good because that’s what we need more of — people who refuse to accept the status quo, who aren’t afraid to take on the unacceptable.”
  • “On the other hand, wasn’t that the very definitely of life? Constant adaptations brought about by a series of never-ending mistakes?”

Yellowface — R.F. Kuang:

  • This was an interesting take on jealousy in friendships + race as an identity; ultimately painting that you will believe what you tell yourself, so be careful what you tell yourself
  • “Writing is the closest thing we have to real magic”
  • “But the best revenge is to thrive.”
  • “Social media is such a tiny, insular space. Once you close your screen, no one gives a fuck.”
  • “What more can we want as writers than such immortality? Don’t ghosts just want to be remembered?”

Tomorrow and Tomorrow and Tomorrow — Gabrielle Zevin:

  • explores platonic relationships in a deep way, how craft and work can be interwoven in, interesting because most times the common advice is to keep work out of friendship unless of course you can combine the two, then the passion compounds
  • “What is a game?” Marx said. “It’s tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow. It’s the possibility of infinite rebirth, infinite redemption. The idea that if you keep playing, you could win. No loss is permanent, because nothing is permanent, ever.”
  • “And what is love, in the end?” Alabaster said. “Except the irrational desire to put evolutionary competitiveness aside in order to ease someone else’s journey through life?”
  • “Sam’s doctor said to him, “The good news is that the pain is in your head.”
    But I am in my head, Sam thought.”
  • “If you’re always aiming for perfection, you won’t make anything at all.”
  • “To Marx, it seemed foolish not to love as many things as you could.”
  • “It was never worth worrying about someone you didn’t love. And it wasn’t love if you didn’t worry.”
  • “He loved the intimacy of being in a tight group of people who had come together, miraculously, for a brief period in time, for the purpose of making art.”
  • “Friendship,” Marx said, “is kind of like having a Tamagotchi.”

Sunrise on the Reaping — Suzanne Collins:

  • “And that’s part of our trouble. Thinking things are inevitable. Not believing change is possible.”

The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes — Suzanne Collins:

  • “Before need, before love, came trust.”
  • “What young brains lack in experience they sometimes make up for in idealism. Nothing seems impossible to them.”
  • “Nothing you can take from me was ever worth keeping.”

title: The Thwaites Glacier: Nature’s Doomsday Domino
excerpt: Human driven activity in the past few years has led to an exponential increase in greenhouse gasses, such as carbon dioxide, that absorb and trap heat in Ear…
content:
Human driven activity in the past few years has led to an exponential increase in greenhouse gasses, such as carbon dioxide, that absorb and trap heat in Earth’s Atmosphere.

This has led to the global climate to increase in temperature causing ice in the Earth’s poles to melt. The Thwaites glacier in the western portion of Antarctica is currently responsible for 4% of the global sea level rise and that number is set to increase.

Glaciers are slowly moving masses of ice and part of the ice that floats on the water is called the ice shelf. The Thwaites glacier stretches 120 km across the west coast of Antarctica with its bedrock resting around 1000 m below sea level. Western Antarctica is largely below sea level and unlike the eastern portion, the area is more susceptible to warming sea water that can easily go underneath the ice. And as the ice shelf shrinks, the grounding line recedes.

The grounding point is the place where the glacier rests on the bed rock. Thwaite’s grounding line has been retreating due to the warm ocean water going underneath the ice shelf and has moved 14 km since 1992. In short, ice that used to be on land, is now on water more prone to melting. All of this results in an increase in the amount of ice breaking off and flowing away from Thwaites, which has already doubled over the past 30 years.

Researchers have also discovered a large cavity in the glacier that’s about the size of ⅔ of Manhattan which also increases the speed at which the glacier is melting. Furthermore, due to its location, the the glacier melting would open up the rest of western antarctica to an increase in melting resulting in a whopping 3 m sea level rise across the globe that could submerge parts of Miami, New York, the Netherlands, and Bangladesh.

We can potentially slow and stall this collapse but it will require significant changes. The way we govern and live on this planet as well as our priorities all need to shift in order to preserve the future of our planet. This means controlling and curbing the amount of greenhouse gas emissions that are causing climate change. The Thwaites glacier embodies a doomsday domino. Whether or not that domino tips and falls in the near future causes destructive and permanent impacts depends on us because once it starts, like a domino, it’s not going to stop.

title: Periods, and why the topic shouldn’t stop conversations.
excerpt: So first off, I want you to imagine this:
content:
So first off, I want you to imagine this:

A young girl looks around a classroom before slowly unzipping her backpack. She quickly transfers an unknown object from her backpack into her pocket. Slipping outside of class, in fast swift motions she makes her way through a couple hallways with hands crammed in pockets. With this description, one would assume that the girl is hiding something suspicious but the truth is she is simply on her period.

In fact, many young girls, especially those who just started menstruating have been through a similar situation. Where they are told to hide a natural process that half the population goes through as if it’s some shameful secret, where pads and tampons are treated like grenades. Where young girls have a fear of saying that they need a feminine hygiene product, which is a medical product I might add, and instead settle for raising eyebrows and saying, “do you have a… you know?”

Stigma surrounding menstruation is rooted deep in our society and affects the way we talk about it and more importantly educate young women and men alike. This hinders conversations surrounding the strides that need to be made in order to improve women’s health. Many people around the world, especially those who live below the poverty line, do not have access to public sanitation facilities and feminine hygiene products, both of which are necessary to health. This extends to education not only about menstruation but also in general as girls across the world miss school and activities or even drop out due to not having proper resources to take care of their periods. So unlike the punctuation, the topic of periods should not halt conversations but rather serve as a starting off point to de-stigmatize menstruation.

The Basics:

Now before I get ahead of myself, Let’s first ask the question: What is menstruation? Menstruation, or periods, is normal vaginal bleeding that occurs as part of a person’s monthly cycle. Every month, a woman’s body prepares for pregnancy. If no pregnancy occurs, the uterus, or womb, sheds its lining. The menstrual blood is partly blood and partly tissue from inside the uterus. Typically, this bleeding lasts anywhere between 3 to 8 days averaging around 5 days. And a woman will spend about 6 years of her life menstruating.

Now this process is completely natural, at least 49.6% of the population have gone through it. Yet, as I mentioned before, menstruation is rarely discussed openly, and is often seen as something shameful that a person must hide or something that makes them dirty, untouchable, or impure.

I’m sure we’ve all heard the many code words we have used, from “aunt flow” and the “crimson tide” to even “the curse”. In fact, a study conducted by the International Women’s Health Coalition found that there are about 5,000 slang words used to refer to menstruation in just 10 different languages which is indicative of the stigma surrounding the topic. Although some of these have been praised for their creative and inventive nature, they show the shame associated with periods.

Forms of discrimination:

So now how exactly does this stigma surrounding menstruation manifest?

Well a lack of conversation around periods is often fueled by detrimental concepts and beliefs about menstruation that have been developed by cultures all over the world. For example, myths such as “Being on your period means that you can infect other people or food” or that “using a tampon causes you to lose your virginity” are completely false. But the perpetuation of such ideas leads to discrimination as women are denied basic human rights and dignity which may take the form of being excluded from everyday physical spaces or being forced into solitary confinement.

In some places in Venezuela, many women are forced to sleep in huts for the duration of their menstruation. In Nepal, this practice is known as Chapudi which was banned in 2017 after deaths had occurred, but even then the practice of isolating menstruating women and girls still continues. In some parts of rural Ghana, menstruating women are forbidden from entering a house with a man or cooking food. In some communities in Burundi, it is thought that contact with a woman who has her period can be deadly for family members. These forms of discrimination show how women are looked down upon because of menstruation, as it is seen as a form of weakness rather than a necessary biological function.

[A Lack] of Education about periods:

On the note of education, The misinformation and beliefs that fuel this discrimination are often taught to future generations. And sometimes period stigma results in teaching nothing at all. A study in Nairobi’s Mathare Valley slum found that over 75% of girls had no idea what menstruation was before they got their first period leading to misinformation and feelings of fear and anxiety when it happened. According to Plan International, 43% of young girls surveyed had no idea what to do when their period first started.

Now I’m sure we’ve all heard or seen the “joke” of a girl getting her first period and thinking that she’s going to die. But laughs aside, imagine being in that girl’s shoes and truly not knowing anything about menstruation and all of a sudden you see blood. That situation, that fear, the uncertainty, is a very real reality for many young girls across the world. Furthermore, just like the lack of sex education, the lack of menstrual education, leads to unsafe practices because of simply not knowing. And societal pressures make it harder for some people to seek out what they don’t know.

Now, let’s talk about the lack of access to resources:

I want you all to imagine again, about a time when you got your period, and was unprepared. For those of you who don’t necessarily have this experience, let me paint you mine. For me, it happened while hiking, I was on my 5th mile when I started to notice something was off. And almost instantly a sense of panic and dread came as I grew anxious, “did I bleed through”, “where’s the nearest restroom”, “shoot, I forgot the pads’ ‘. These were the questions that were going through my mind. Now thankfully, a mile later I was able to find a restroom with feminine hygiene products and the sense of relief was immense. But, for many women around the world, the feeling of dread and anxiety and panic without the relief of finding resources is something they go through every single month. The lack of education combined with the lack of access to public sanitation facilities and feminine hygiene products leads to the inability to properly take care of one’s menstrual health especially for the 12.8% of women and girls that live in poverty.

Toilets:

According to “We Can’t Wait”, a report about sanitation facilities for women and girls around the world. About 1.2 billion women/girls have no access to a safe, private toilet and 526 million don’t have access to a toilet at all. A toilet is something we all take for granted, but it is necessary for public sanitation and the lack of one could result in some to resort to public defecation. This makes people more prone to disease and harassment. The lack of access to proper sanitation facilities, clean water, combined with stigma systematically sidelines women’s needs. According to researchers at UC Berkeley’s Blum Centers for Developing Economies, many portable restrooms that are built in rural communities are designed solely to keep fecal matter out of the water supply and are not accommodating to pregnant or menstruating women. As a result, many young girls often will toss their menstrual hygiene products into the latrine, which can cause clogging, or will throw it into nature, which is not an environmentally sustainable practice. In addition, many females will put their used products into their bags and carry them around, which is unhygienic.

Which brings us to another point, feminine hygiene products:

Menstrual products, despite being a necessity, are still expensive, are often taxed, and leave many women struggling to take care of their cycles safely. They may even resort to using unsanitary items such as newspapers, plastic bags, and even rags which make them extremely prone to contracting diseases. In the US for example, Feminine hygiene products are taxed as “luxury items” and not necessities thus, subject to a sales tax ranging from 4 to 10 percent. Overall, Many young women don’t have access to any type of sanitary pad, which can cause them to miss school and cause them to be 70% more likely to have reproductive tract infections.

Which brings us to the point of schooling:

In Kenya, studies have shown that girls will miss an average of four days of school each month — adding up to about 20% of the school year. This is replicated across the world and exacerbated when schools don’t have safe, clean, and properly working toilets.

Why should you care?

Regardless if you menstruate or not, the stigma surrounding it affects you or someone you know. Breaking these barriers can help significantly reform school environments and work environments allowing for women to better their health and increase their economic opportunities. Not to mention legislation benefits more than just women, everyone benefits from clean safe bathrooms and more inclusive legislation can cause a ripple effect in awareness about similar topics.

So what can we do?

Building restrooms with menstruating women in mind can help solve many of these issues that often act as a barrier for women to gain education and employment, and these solutions may ultimately be able to address gender inequities in developing countries all around the world. Through increasing education, promoting female empowerment, providing expanded resources, and encouraging candid conversations surrounding women’s health, we can work towards destigmatizing menstruation. Reaching out to policy makers regarding these issues can help make strides to implement more legislation with menstrual health in mind. And lastly we can change that way we think and act around the topic of menstruation starting with calling periods for what they are, a completely natural process.

References:

https://medlineplus.gov/menstruation.html

https://blogs.unicef.org/blog/10-period-lies/

https://pha.berkeley.edu/2018/06/05/menstruation-stigma-must-stop-period/

title: Our People — A Poem
content:
Our People — A Poem

By: Sanjana Taware

We only have people, they’re all we’ve got.
Listen to our stories, even the ones that are quoted, “boring.”
Be our shoulders to cry on, a lap to rest our heads.
They tell you off when we’re not yourself, and are the first ones to welcome us back.
So it is no surprise that when they leave… We are broken.

We are more connected and more subjected to the opinion of others.
Every critique is written in ink not pencil, stored forever in 1’s and 0’s.
Internet shaming is here, forget the scarlet letter.
A sense of euphoria instilled through hits of social media.
Associating our fictitious usernames with the filtered faces.

Yet, the people that we hold the closest to us go beyond these social formalities.
And in times of distress, disappointment, devastation.
Where doubt fills the gaps in increasingly bare conversation.
Give each other a chance to fill those lapses with trust, with people, they’re all we’ve got.

title: Ouch! — Flash Fiction
content:
Ouch! — Flash Fiction

By Sanjana Taware

Arjun’s makeshift towel cape flew in the wind as he stood on top of a bright blue neighborhood wall that bordered the apartment complex where he lived. A ginger cat on the same wall slyly kept towards a bird’s nest perched in a nearby tree filled with baby birds. In broad swinging-like motions, he used his “karate chops” to scare away the cat. By his side was Pia, his best friend since they both could walk. She was his sidekick, always partaking in antics together. Most of the time, their adventures would be of Pia’s imagination that would run wild, concocting fantastical missions where they both would save the day. Pia wanted to save the birds and it was Arjun’s idea to climb up onto the wall to do it, after all, heights didn’t scare him. They were an inseparable pair and as they manically waved their arms with determination, both had wide smiles on their faces. The cat scurried away leaving the baby bird’s nest safe for one more day.

Another mission complete, Arjun thought to himself. He jumped down to the ground with his fist jutting out towards the air superman style. He landed with a large thud on his knees but the impact didn’t cause a single wince on his face. The pain was nothing to Arjun. A young boy no more than six years old, he had already understood what his rare genetic condition meant. He was born with congenital insensitivity to pain, a rare disorder that creates an inability to feel pain and temperature. This was Arjun’s superpower as he became the bravest person in his school. He made sure to be careful to not do anything that would hurt his health, but when it came to killing spiders or fighting off evil hungry cats, Arjun was the first to jump, sometimes quite literally, to the rescue.

“Arjun!” called his mom, Mamta, as she rushed out to the wall and quickly picked up Arjun, inspecting every inch of him for any bruises, scrapes, or broken bones as she muttered to herself and her brows furrowed in worried concern.

“Mumma, don’t worry,” Arjun assured with a giggling smile, “I remember what you always say, ‘to be careful.’ The wall is only three feet, I knew nothing was going to happen to me, but those baby birds — ”

Pia’s sobs cut off Arjun’s explanation. “Pia, are you okay?” Mamta asked frantically, turning her attention towards her. Arjun also turned to his friend Pia who had jumped alongside him except she was still on the ground. Tears began to well in her eyes as Mamta was trying to comfort her. She had no visible scratches, sprains, or injuries as Arjun could see. He couldn’t understand why she was crying. She was clutching her elbow with her other hand.

“I didn’t land on my feet Mamta Auntie,” Pia exclaimed between sniffles.

“There there,” Mamta soothingly said, “You’ll be fine soon batcha, thankfully nothing was seriously injured.” Arjun made his way to Pia to hug her and help her up as all three of them went home.

Once home and Pia’s mom had picked her daughter up, Mamta turned to Arjun with a concerned look. “Arjun, you won’t feel pain, but that doesn’t mean it is not there, that it can’t hurt you, and others.” Arjun stared down at the floor with sad eyes, the last thing he ever wanted to do was hurt his friends, he thought, that’s not what superheroes do. Mamta sighed and after looking at Arjun’s red cape gently smiled reassuringly. She crouched down beside Arjun, lifting his head. “Think of this as another one of your superpowers, even if you don’t feel pain, you must imagine how it feels and act accordingly. It’s like…another sense similar to how I and Pia can feel when someone does this.” Mamta tapped her arm then. Arjun then put his hand on his mom’s forearm.

“Ouch!” Arjun exclaimed.

Mamta, smiling a bit wider this time, “Ouch is correct,” agreed.

The next day, Arjun went to Pia’s house to ask to play. As Pia opened the door, he saw she was feeling much better, ready to go on another adventure. At that moment they both heard a crash coming from the bright blue wall. The same cat was trying to infiltrate the bird’s nest once again. Arjun and Pia rushed to the rescue, reaching the base of the wall, ready to climb it. Pia looked nervously at the wall, clutching her elbow with her other hand. Arjun looked at her and then backed up at the cat. Even if I can climb the wall, it doesn’t mean others can, he thought to himself.

“I have a new idea,” Arjun exclaimed, as he picked up a stick from the ground. Pia smiled as she quickly understood the plan. With small tree branches in hand, Arjun and Pia warded off the cat from the safety of the ground. While Arjun couldn’t utilize his “karate skills” he found using his new superpowers of understanding what others might feel, and his wooden stick wizardry, with Pia by his side, saved the day just as well.

title: To The Limit — Children’s Story
content:
To The Limit — Children’s Story

By Sanjana Taware

Daniel the Delivery Truck spent his days traversing downtown avenues and winding neighborhood streets to make his scheduled deliveries for the U.S. Post Office alongside his driver. On chilly evenings in December, everything from classic carols to holiday-inspired dance numbers blasted from Daniel’s speakers. Children would come running to their windows filled with the anticipation of holiday presents and excitement as they sang along to the tunes as he drove by.

It was almost as if Daniel was an ice cream truck, and while he wasn’t carrying delicious treats, he took great pride in his job. Each package or letter brought joy to the recipients, and his role was instrumental in making that possible. This was all the more clear through the kid’s smiles gleaming through the window and the laughter from the dog walker and joggers that he passed by on his delivery route.

As one of the U.S. Post Office’s best delivery trucks, he was responsible for transporting packages and letters no matter the weather. Rain, snow, or shine, his engine was powerful, his GPS was reliable, and he never failed to deliver on time. Daniel adhered to a strict schedule set forth by the post office each morning. Nate, his usual driver, would come into work with the day’s list of deliveries on his handy clipboard. He developed the habit of speaking to himself while driving long hours and Daniel would use his murmurs to guide him.

One Monday in Autumn, Nate came into work with a longer list than usual. “Today’s schedule looks pretty tight since some of the other trucks had to be sent for repairs. No time for stops if I want to make it to my husband’s office party,” Nate said to himself as he started Daniel’s engine. Daniel understood Nate’s need and adjusted accordingly. No stops meant no stops, Daniel confirmed.

This wasn’t unusual for Daniel. He was used to picking up extra deliveries when other trucks were unable to. He prided himself in being able to drive long distances with little pit stops through rough weather. His dependability made him the truck to turn to for extra deliveries, especially with the increasing demand at the post office he worked for.

Midway through the schedule Daniel’s engine started to overheat. This was usually the point where he would turn on his Engine Temperature Warning Light. It was a bright red sign on Nate’s steering wheel to signal to him a break was needed. This time, however, Daniel hesitated. He thought of Nate’s need to get back in time to be there for his husband and more crucially all the packages that could go undelivered if Daniel didn’t utilize his full engine endurance. I can take the hard work, he thought, I have to. The warning light flashed for only a second before Daniel shut it off himself.

As Daniel drove over the long stretches of the highways, his wheels turned at a faster and more consistent pace, completing twice the amount of deliveries in the time it takes to do half. When he and Nate pulled back into the U.S. Post Office, Nate exclaimed to himself, “Just in time for the office party! No technical issues, no pit stops! What a productive day!” It was comments like those that gave Daniel satisfaction as he cooled his engine for the night.

The next day, Nate strode into the office with his clipboard and a list of Tuesday’s deliveries. This list was even longer than Monday’s. As Nate scanned the route he exclaimed, “Man, with all the new houses and businesses being built in the city, these lists just keep getting longer and longer. I guess that means longer shifts but luckily I’m driving the best truck in the company.”

To complete the schedule, Daniel ran his engine until it was piping hot and powered through freezing rain by ferociously waving his windshield wipers. His tires roughly grounded against the paved roads as they turned with determination. Through the cold, harsh weather, his battery simultaneously powered his heater and bright headlights. He made all the deliveries, he was a delivery truck after all.

This pattern continued every day with a list that was longer than the day before. Every time Daniel pushed his limits, Nate was able to complete more deliveries so when he turned in his clipboard at the end of the night, the post office kept adding more to increase their profits and efficiency. Daniel’s hours extended beyond Nate’s shifts so Shreya and Andre also became his drivers. His engine started even earlier in the morning and switched it off even later at night.

Within a few weeks but what felt like forever to Daniel, Winter arrived. The usual light decorations that made Daniel’s delivery routes festive, blurred together in a bright haze as his wheels spun quickly to complete yet another long list of deliveries.

As he turned the corner of the last stretch of his delivery route, his GPS detected some familiarity. This was one of his usual routes around the holidays, one where Nate would turn on Daniel’s stereo and cheerful music would blast through the air. Thankfully, Nate was Daniel’s driver at that moment. Out of habit, Nate reached for the stereo to turn it on but not a single sound came out of the speakers. Daniel tried to play the songs that would bring smiles to the children’s faces. He tried to muster up the energy to turn on his sound system. But, his battery was too drained from cooling his overheated engine, running his heater, and shining his headlights the entire day.

As the street with the festive lights faded away, Daniel felt empty. With his fuel tank nearly depleted, when he turned off his engine, the satisfaction and even joy that he usually felt was gone. He had completed all his deliveries, so what was wrong? he thought. Playing music, one of his favorite parts of his job, was gone. His focus had shifted to satisfying the post office’s, Nate’s, and his other driver’s goals, but in the process, his motivation seemed to be as blurred as the lights of the buildings he drove by. He was the best delivery truck, but he wasn’t his best, he realized. Daniel decided to adjust the way he approached his schedule this time using his own limits to guide him.

The next day Nate strode in saying to himself as he started Daniel’s engine, “Got a long list of deliveries today, better get a move on!” A quarter of a way through the deliveries, Daniel felt his engine overheating and this time did not ignore it.

He turned on his Engine Temperature Warning Light. As the red signal flashed in front of Nate. Nate stared at the indicator and pulled over to the side. He entered a rest stop into the GPS and started driving towards it. “Better to be safe than sorry, I’ll take a quick break, and let the engine cool off a bit,” Nate said to himself.

Throughout the day, Daniel used his warning lights and monitors to make sure the schedule didn’t deplete his battery. Although he didn’t finish all the extra deliveries assigned to him as quickly, by taking his time, he knew that each delivery was completed correctly and he enjoyed himself along the way. He had done his job in a way that made him proud as he turned off his engine for the night.

Before Nate left that night, Daniel overheard him mumble, “Hmm, today needed a lot more rest stops than usual, I’ll have to let the post office know about that to get a less difficult schedule to accommodate for that.”

The next day, Nate came in with a modified list with fewer deliveries for Daniel to complete so that he didn’t strain himself. While his responsibilities never completely changed, Daniel did. He realized that doing his job didn’t require pushing himself beyond his limits and hurting himself in the process. It was important for him to make sure that didn’t happen. Now when the holidays came, he knew he would be able to blast tunes that brought joy to both the children he passed and more importantly, himself.
